title: Manufacturing and Quality Control: From Forging to Final Inspection
description: A nozzle flange starts as an ingot. A massive chunk of raw steel or superalloy, often weighing thousands of pounds. This ingot is placed in a furnace and hea...
content:
What Happens Inside the Vault: The Forging Process

A nozzle flange starts as an ingot. A massive chunk of raw steel or superalloy, often weighing thousands of pounds. This ingot is placed in a furnace and heated to around 2,000-2,200°F—hot enough that the metal becomes plastic and can be shaped.

Once the ingot is at temperature, it is moved to the forging press. A hydraulic or mechanical hammer repeatedly strikes the metal, compressing it and shaping it. The flange face is formed. The hub is shaped. The neck is drawn down to its final dimensions.

Each blow of the hammer does more than just shape the part. It consolidates internal voids, aligns grain boundaries, and increases density. A forged nozzle is far stronger and more reliable than a casting because the grain structure has been mechanically aligned by the forging process.

After forging, the part is allowed to cool—carefully, to avoid cracking from thermal shock. Then it goes to the machine shop for finish machining.

Quality Assurance: Every Step Is Tested

The ingot is tested. Chemical composition is verified. The forged part is tested. The machined part is tested. The final part is tested again.

Standard tests include:

Tensile testing: A sample of material is pulled until it fails. The yield strength and ultimate tensile strength are recorded. Impact testing: A notched sample (usually at -20°F for sour service) is struck by a pendulum hammer. The energy absorbed before failure (impact toughness) is measured. Hardness testing: A small indenter is pressed into the surface, and the size of the impression is measured as hardness. Liquid penetrant testing (PT): The part is covered with a fluorescent dye, then cleaned. The dye remains in any surface cracks. Under UV light, defects are visible. Ultrasonic testing (UT): Sound waves are passed through the material to detect internal voids or inclusions. Radiography (X-ray): Used on large forgings to detect internal defects.

All of this data is compiled into a Material Test Report (MTR) and delivered with the part.

Pressure Decay and Helium Leak Testing: For Critical Applications

For ultra-critical components (like those going into aerospace or medical applications), manufacturers sometimes perform helium leak testing. The part is placed in a chamber, pressurized with helium, and connected to a sensitive mass spectrometer that detects minute leaks.

This is overkill for most industrial pressure vessel applications, but for certain sour service components, it can justify the cost.

Dimensional Verification: Tolerance Control

Every critical dimension is measured and documented. Bore diameter, hub height, flange ID, flange OD, bolt hole positions—everything. If a dimension is out of tolerance, the part is either reworked by machining or rejected.

This is where quality control either catches problems or lets junk through. A badly-controlled shop might measure things loosely and let parts pass that should be rejected. A rigorous shop will measure multiple points and average, ensuring repeatability.

Surface Finish: Critical for Corrosion Resistance

A rough surface finish on a nozzle destined for corrosive service is a red flag. Roughness creates crevices where corrosion bacteria thrive. It also increases susceptibility to stress corrosion cracking.

Specification often call for surface finish no rougher than 63 microinches RMS (root mean square). In sour service, 32 microinches or better is preferred. This requires fine grinding or polishing after machining.

Deviation Reports and Documentation

Sometimes, a part does not quite meet specification. Maybe the hardness is 0.5 points higher than the maximum allowed. Maybe the tensile strength is 3 ksi above the specification limit. Maybe a dimension is 0.005 inches out of tolerance.

When deviation occurs, the manufacturer issues a "deviation report" or "material review note" (MRN) and submits it for engineering approval. Engineering makes a call: Does the deviation affect fitness for service? If no, the part is approved with a documented waiver. If yes, the part is rejected or required to undergo rework.

Do not just ignore deviations. A seemingly minor deviation might compound with other variables to create a risk. This is where engineering judgment and experience matter.

The Bottom Line

A quality forging and flange manufacturing facility follows strict procedures at every step. They understand that poor quality in the factory becomes poor performance in the field. A reliable vendor will have strong QC procedures, extensive material test documentation, and the willingness to reject parts that do not meet specification. That is the vendor you want.
